model_narrative: We are given a graph whose edges carry weights, and we must split its nodes into two sides. For each node we decide which of the two sides it goes on, and an edge is said to cross the cut when its two endpoints end up on opposite sides. We also track, for each edge, whether it crosses the cut, and we track the total weight collected from all crossing edges. The goal is to choose the partition so that the total weight of the edges crossing the cut is as large as possible.

sets.name: n
sets.doc: the nodes (vertices) of the graph, indexed by name

sets.name: e
sets.doc: the edges of the graph, given as ordered node pairs (i, j) with i listed before j; each pair connects two distinct nodes

params.name: w
params.doc: the weight of each edge, which contributes to the cut value only when that edge has its two endpoints on opposite sides of the partition; weights may be positive or negative

vars.name: x
vars.doc: the side of the partition that each node is assigned to, taking value 0 for one side and 1 for the other

vars.name: cut
vars.doc: the cut indicator of each edge, intended to be 1 when the edge's two endpoints lie on opposite sides and 0 otherwise; relaxed to the continuous interval from 0 to 1, since the surrounding inequalities and the maximizing objective drive it to the correct integral value

vars.name: z
vars.doc: the total weight of all edges that cross the cut

constraints.name: obj_def
constraints.intent: The total cut weight must equal the combined weight of every edge that crosses the cut, where each edge contributes its own weight scaled by how much it crosses. Add up that contribution across all edges and set the tracked total equal to it.

constraints.name: cut[i,
constraints.intent: An edge can only count as crossing the cut if at least one of its two endpoints sits on the side labelled one. For each edge, its crossing indicator must not exceed the combined side labels of its two endpoints, so when both endpoints are on side zero the edge cannot cross.

constraints.name: cut[i,
constraints.intent: An edge can only count as crossing the cut if at least one of its two endpoints sits on the side labelled zero. For each edge, its crossing indicator must not exceed the amount by which the two endpoint side labels fall short of both being on side one, so when both endpoints are on side one the edge cannot cross.

constraints.name: cut[i,
constraints.intent: Whenever the first endpoint of an edge is on side one and the second is on side zero, that edge must be counted as crossing the cut. For each edge, its crossing indicator must be at least the first endpoint's side label minus the second endpoint's side label.

constraints.name: cut[i,
constraints.intent: Whenever the second endpoint of an edge is on side one and the first is on side zero, that edge must be counted as crossing the cut. For each edge, its crossing indicator must be at least the second endpoint's side label minus the first endpoint's side label.

query: Nodes 1 and 2 are two halves of one physical unit that has to stay together in the same group. If we require them to land on the same side of the cut, what total cut weight does the optimizer report?

query: Every edge that crosses the partition costs us a cable run to maintain, and we can only support so many of them. Suppose we hold the number of edges crossing the cut to at most 400 — what cut weight can we still achieve under that ceiling?
expected_pyomo: model.edgecap = Constraint(expr=sum(model.cut[i, j] for (i, j) in model.e) <= 400)

query: There's a hard requirement that nodes 1 and 3 sit in opposite groups for redundancy. If we force those two nodes onto different sides of the cut, where does the total cut weight land?

query: The first five nodes belong to one tightly-coupled subsystem that has to be kept whole on a single side. If we pin nodes 1 through 5 all to the same group, what's the resulting cut weight?
expected_pyomo:
model.block_lo = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[str(k)] for k in range(1, 6)) >= 5 * model.x['1'])
model.block_hi = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[str(k)] for k in range(1, 6)) <= 5 * model.x['1'])

query: Nodes 2 and 3 are mirrored backups of each other and policy says they must share a group. If we require them on the same side of the cut, what total weight does the optimizer come back with?

query: Nodes 2 and 5 belong to the same critical service group, yet the plan splits them apart to collect cut weight. Shouldn't they really be kept together? Force them onto the same side and tell me what the model is trading off.

query: Nodes 3 and 4 sit at the ends of one of the strongest links in the network, but the solution still pulls them onto opposite sides. Couldn't we just keep that pair together instead? What does the optimizer lose if we make them share a side?

query: Nodes 1 and 5 are kept on the same side in the plan, but operationally they're rivals that should never share a group. Wouldn't it be cleaner to just put them in separate groups? What's the model seeing that makes keeping them together worth it?

query: Nodes 4 and 5 are joined by a very heavy edge, and the partition deliberately separates them to bank that weight. From an operational view I'd rather they stayed in the same group — shouldn't they? Pin them together and show me what the cut gives up.
